title: Airbnb Host Retention Optimization
subtitle: Allocating $1,000 retention gifts to maximize profit, not accuracy

headlineMetric.label: platform profit uplift on a 1,312-host test set

summary: Built a profit-aware decision system that increased net profit by $557K by gifting only the 23.4% of hosts where retention dollars actually pay back.
problem: Airbnb wanted to send $1,000 gifts to hosts at risk of leaving the platform. The trap: a high-accuracy churn model isn't the same as a profitable intervention. Gifting low-revenue hosts loses money even if you correctly predict they'll churn.
approach: Engineered features from 12-month reservation trends, revenue projections, and K-Means geographic clustering (20 clusters)
Trained a CatBoost ensemble across 8 random seeds with 5-fold cross-validation
Applied isotonic calibration to convert model scores into honest probability estimates
Optimized a profit threshold (τ* = $1,082.96): gift only when attrition_probability × annual_profit exceeds threshold
insights: Gifting only 307 of 1,312 hosts (23.4%) outperforms gifting everyone or no one
The profit-at-risk framing prioritizes high-revenue at-risk hosts over equally-likely-to-churn low-revenue hosts
Geographic clustering meaningfully improved feature signal beyond raw lat/lon
impact: Increased projected platform profit from $5,239,448 to $5,796,773 — a +$557,325 uplift, or ~10.6% on the 1,312-host test set. Model AUC was 0.9025, but the real win was reframing the objective from accuracy to dollars.

title: Customer Segmentation & Churn Prediction
subtitle: Identifying the 28% of customers who drive 83% of revenue

headlineMetric.label: of revenue concentrated in top 28.9% of customers

summary: Segmented 5,878 customers from 779K transactions using RFM features and predicted churn risk to enable targeted retention campaigns.
problem: An online retailer needed to know which customers to invest retention dollars in. Treating all customers the same wastes budget; treating them by gut feel misses revenue. The task was to segment customers by value and behavior, then predict who was likely to churn.
approach: Cleaned 1.07M transactions (UCI Online Retail II) down to 779,425 rows across 5,878 customers
Engineered 13 customer-level features blending RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) with behavioral and temporal signals
Ran K-Means with silhouette analysis to settle on 3 segments (silhouette = 0.5748)
Built a churn classifier on top of the segments to flag at-risk customers
insights: 1,700 'High-Value Loyal' customers (28.9%) drive 82.8% of revenue — average spend £8,467
2,342 'Growth/Mid-Tier' customers represent the highest conversion opportunity
1,836 'At-Risk/Dormant' customers contribute only 5.9% of revenue — win-back ROI must be carefully scoped
impact: The segmentation enables retention spend to be concentrated on high-value customers rather than spread thin, and the churn model (AUC-ROC 0.7716) gives marketing a prioritized list to act on weekly.

title: Multi-Agent Financial Decision System
subtitle: AI agents that simulate a finance committee

headlineMetric.label: of reasoning errors caught by adversarial agent review

summary: Built a LangGraph system coordinating Strategy, Risk, Finance, and Modeling agents to produce scenario-based corporate finance recommendations.
problem: Corporate finance decisions (build vs. buy, capacity expansion, investment sizing) require multiple lenses — strategic fit, risk tolerance, financial returns, modeling rigor — that humans usually run in silos. Single-LLM approaches collapse this into one voice and lose the adversarial check.
approach: Architected a LangGraph state machine with four specialized agents: Strategy, Risk, Finance, and Modeling
Added an assumption-governance layer so each agent's inputs are explicit and traceable
Implemented adversarial Risk review: every recommendation gets stress-tested before being surfaced
Generated three-scenario outputs (Downside / Base / Upside) plus sensitivity analyses on key drivers
Built evaluation metrics for decision-quality (consistency, traceability, adversarial coverage)
insights: Adversarial agent review caught reasoning errors that a single-pass LLM missed in ~30% of test cases
Forcing explicit assumption declaration made the model's outputs auditable in a way single-prompt approaches aren't
Three-scenario output (vs. point estimate) shifts the conversation from 'what's the answer' to 'where's the risk'
impact: Produces go/no-go recommendations with the structure of a real finance committee memo — explicit assumptions, scenario sensitivity, and risk flags — at a fraction of the time. Demonstrates how multi-agent systems can encode consulting-style structured thinking.

title: Bankruptcy Prediction Ensemble
subtitle: Stacked gradient-boosting model for financial distress

headlineMetric.label: AUC stacked ensemble on 8K companies × 64 ratios

summary: Two-level stacking ensemble (LightGBM + XGBoost + CatBoost → Logistic Regression) reaching 0.9056 AUC on 8,000 companies × 64 financial ratios.
problem: Predict whether a company will go bankrupt from 64 anonymized financial ratios — a high-stakes binary classification problem where false negatives are expensive (missed defaults) and false positives are expensive (over-conservative lending).
approach: Built a missing-value-aware pipeline: binary null flags + median imputation + robust (IQR) scaling
Added 5 PCA components and 10 interaction terms to capture non-linear feature relationships
Bayesian-tuned three base learners (LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost) with 5-fold stratified CV
Stacked out-of-fold predictions through a regularized Logistic Regression meta-learner (C=0.1) to prevent leakage
insights: Stacked ensemble (0.9056 AUC) beat best base learner (LightGBM 0.9042) — small but real gain
Engineered missingness flags were among the top features — informative missingness mattered
Robust scaling outperformed standard scaling on this dataset's heavy-tailed ratios
impact: Demonstrates that ensemble stacking adds value even when base learners are already strong, and that careful feature engineering (especially around missingness) often beats marginal model tuning.
